intro: The clearest difference between Fork and Linear is pricing — Fork is subscription (no free tier), while Linear is freemium (has a free tier). Beyond that, lock-in — Fork has low lock-in (Git GUI, no data lock-in), whereas Linear has low lock-in (Issue tracker, data exportable). Fork fits mobile; Linear fits SaaS and e-commerce teams managing user journeys. On the strengths side, Fork is cited for Automated build/test/deploy pipelines, while Linear is cited for centralizes customer data and automates lifecycle messaging. The honest trade-off: Fork is the wrong call for hobby projects; Linear is the wrong call for single-product apps with no ongoing user engagement; Fork users flag manual deployment is sufficient, and Linear users flag contact-based pricing scales steeply with list growth. Choosing between them should come down to the constraint that actually binds your workflow, not surface-level overlap. On developer experience the data shows Fork at 4/5 and Linear at 5/5, with transparency at 4/5 and 4/5 respectively.
quick_take: Fork is for mobile; Linear is for SaaS and e-commerce teams managing user; decide based on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Fork if your use case is mobile, and a subscription (no free tier) pricing shape fits your budget better than freemium (has a free tier).
verdict_b: Choose Linear if your use case is SaaS and e-commerce teams managing user journeys, and a freemium (has a free tier) pricing shape fits your budget better than subscription (no free tier).

faq.a: Fork is subscription, while Linear is freemium with a free tier, so the cheaper option depends on your usage shape. Listed pricing pages usually change, so check both directly before committing. For small teams, the free-tier limits often matter more than headline prices.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Fork to Linear?
faq.a: Migrating from Fork to Linear is feasible when their scopes overlap, but the work is in re-mapping configuration, data models, and integrations. Fork has low lock-in, so exporting state is typically straightforward. Plan for a dual-run period so you can validate parity before decommissioning.

On our developer-experience score, Linear is rated 5/5 and Fork 4/5, so Linear edges ahead. Scores like this smooth over real differences in docs, CLI feel, and SDK quality, so treat them as a starting point. Spend an afternoon in each before relying on the number.

faq.q: Is Linear a good alternative to Fork?
faq.a: Linear is a reasonable alternative to Fork when Linear fits SaaS and e-commerce teams managing user journeys. If your use case matches Fork's sweet spot — mobile — staying with Fork is probably safer. Fork itself is not meant for hobby projects, which is often where users start shopping for alternatives.
